extracted_information: The web content provides a comparison of five common load balancing algorithms, detailing their mechanisms, suitable use cases, and limitations. It explains how each algorithm distributes incoming requests among multiple servers in a cluster.

features.name: Round Robin
features.description: Distributes requests cyclically to servers. First request to Server 1, second to Server 2, and so on, looping back to the first server after reaching the last.
features.characteristics: Most widely used, easy to implement, easy to understand.
features.use_cases: Best for clusters consisting of servers with identical specifications (CPU, RAM).
features.trade_offs: Does not account for disproportionate server capacities; can lead to overloading a less capable server if capacities differ, as it distributes requests equally regardless of server specs.

features.name: Weighted Round Robin
features.description: Similar to Round Robin but assigns a greater number of requests to servers with higher 'weights'. Weights are assigned beforehand based on server capacity or desired load distribution.
features.use_cases: Ideal for scenarios where servers have disproportionate capacities; for example, if Server 1 has 5x capacity of Server 2, it receives 5 requests for every 1 request Server 2 receives. Can also be used to intentionally give fewer connections to critical application servers.
features.trade_offs: Requires manual pre-assignment of 'weights' to each node.

features.name: Least Connections
features.description: Assigns new incoming connections to the server that currently has the fewest active connections.
features.use_cases: Suitable when client connection durations vary significantly, preventing servers with long-lived connections from being overloaded while others remain underutilized.
features.trade_offs: Does not inherently account for server capacity differences; a server with fewer connections but lower capacity might still be overstressed.

features.name: Weighted Least Connections
features.description: Combines Least Connections with a 'weight' component. The load balancer considers both the current number of connections and the pre-assigned weights (based on capacity) of each server when distributing a new connection.
features.use_cases: Effective for clusters with varying server capacities and dynamic connection loads.
features.trade_offs: Requires manual pre-assignment of 'weights' to each node.

features.name: Random
features.description: Matches clients and servers randomly using an underlying random number generator.
features.use_cases: When the load balancer receives a very large number of requests, this algorithm can distribute requests evenly across nodes. Sufficient for clusters consisting of nodes with similar configurations.
features.trade_offs: Less predictable for small request volumes; may not be optimal for clusters with highly disparate server capacities without additional mechanisms.
